posts.title: Speculative Decoding: LLMs Guess Ahead to Go Faster
posts.subtitle: A small model guesses. A big model checks. Speed without a dumber answer.
posts.summary: Speculative decoding pairs a fast draft model with a big verifier model so LLMs emit several tokens per step instead of one, cutting latency and cost while the target model's output distribution stays mathematically identical.

slides.title: Speculative Decoding
slides.body: The inference trick letting newer, faster model tiers keep up with the smart ones.

slides.title: One Token at a Time
slides.body: LLMs write sequentially. Each token has to wait for the last one to finish before it can even start.
bullets: Bigger model = smarter, but slower
Latency and cost scale with output length
Every token pays the full forward-pass price

slides.title: Two Models, One Job
bullets: A small, cheap draft model guesses several tokens ahead
A big target model checks all those guesses at once
Checking is parallel — writing never was

slides.title: Guess, Then Verify
slides.body: The draft model proposes a run of tokens. The target model scores the whole run in one forward pass, as if it had written it alone.
bullets: Correct guesses: accepted instantly
First wrong guess: rejected, target resamples from there

slides.title: Where You've Already Felt It
slides.body: Fast-tier releases like Claude's speed-optimized variants and rumored tiers like GPT-5.6's Luna lean on tricks like this to cut cost-per-token while keeping the flagship model's judgment.

rows: Up to 5-8 tokens verified per pass
Draft model absorbs most of the compute

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Speed comes from guess-then-verify, not from shrinking the model
Rejection sampling guarantees quality never drops
This is why new 'fast' model tiers can feel instant without feeling dumb

questions.prompt: In speculative decoding, what do the draft and target models each do?
options: The draft model guesses several tokens ahead; the target model verifies them all in one parallel pass
Both models split the vocabulary between them and generate different words
The target model trains the draft model in real time during inference
The draft model permanently replaces the target model once it's accurate enough
questions.explanation: The small draft model proposes a run of tokens; the large target model checks the entire run in a single forward pass and keeps only what it agrees with.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't speculative decoding make outputs lower quality?
options: It quietly lowers the sampling temperature
It only ever outputs tokens the draft model produced
The target model's own probabilities decide acceptance via rejection sampling, so the final output distribution matches running the target model alone
It rounds each response to the nearest cached answer
questions.explanation: Rejection sampling mathematically guarantees the accepted tokens come from the same distribution the target model would have produced on its own.

questions.prompt: What happens when the draft model's guess turns out to be wrong?
options: The entire response restarts from the first token
The target model accepts the correct prefix up to the first wrong token, then resamples the rest itself
The draft model is discarded and never used again
The wrong guess is kept anyway to save time
questions.explanation: Only the verified-correct prefix is kept; generation continues from the target model's own resampled token at the point of disagreement.

posts.title: Choreography vs Orchestration
posts.subtitle: One conductor waving a baton, or dancers who just know their cues.
posts.summary: Orchestration puts a central brain in charge of every step, trading autonomy for visibility and easy debugging. Choreography lets services react to events independently, trading central control for resilience and loose coupling — the same tradeoff now applies to wiring multi-agent pipelines.

Who owns the overall flow?

slides.title: Key takeaways
bullets: Orchestration: centralized control, easy visibility, one point of failure
Choreography: decentralized reactions, resilient, hard to trace
Most real systems (and agent pipelines) blend both
Choose based on how much you need to see vs how much you need to scale

posts.title: HNSW vs IVF: Choosing a Vector Index That Scales
posts.subtitle: Every RAG demo hides a recall-vs-latency bet it hasn't made yet.
posts.summary: HNSW and IVF are both approximate nearest neighbor indexes, but they trade recall, latency, memory, and build cost differently. Pick HNSW for low-latency real-time search and IVF for massive, cost-constrained datasets — then benchmark on your own embeddings.

slides.title: The Index Nobody Benchmarks Until It's Too Late
slides.body: Behind every vector database is an approximate nearest neighbor index — and the choice between HNSW and IVF decides whether your RAG system survives production.

slides.title: Your Demo Worked. Then You Hit 10M Vectors.
slides.body: Brute-force cosine similarity is exact but scans every vector — fine for 10K rows, fatal for 10M. You need an index that trades a little accuracy for a lot of speed.
bullets: Exact search scans every vector
10M vectors = seconds per query
Users expect answers under 100ms

slides.title: Every ANN Index Makes the Same Bet
slides.body: Approximate Nearest Neighbor indexes skip most of the dataset on purpose. HOW they decide what to skip shapes recall, latency, memory, and update cost.
bullets: Recall: % of true neighbors found
Latency: time per query
Memory: index size vs raw data
Build & update cost

slides.title: HNSW: Navigate a Graph of Shortcuts
slides.body: Hierarchical Navigable Small World builds layered graphs — sparse highways on top, dense local links below — so search hops toward your query in near-log time.
bullets: Top layer: long-range jumps
Bottom layer: fine-grained neighbors
High recall, low latency
Memory-hungry, slower to build

slides.title: IVF: Search Only the Right Neighborhood
slides.body: Inverted File indexing clusters vectors into cells, like k-means, then scans only the few cells closest to your query — skipping the rest entirely.
bullets: Vectors clustered into cells
Query touches only nprobe cells
Compact and fast to build
Recall drops near cell edges

slides.title: Same Job, Different Tradeoffs
slides.body: Neither index is strictly better — each optimizes for a different failure mode.

slides.title: Pick the Index, Not the Vibe
bullets: HNSW: real-time agent memory, chat RAG, sub-50ms budgets
IVF (+PQ): billion-scale, cost-constrained, batch-friendly
pgvector, Milvus, Qdrant let you choose per collection
Always benchmark recall@k on your own embeddings

questions.prompt: What does an ANN (Approximate Nearest Neighbor) index trade away to gain speed?
options: Some recall accuracy for lower latency
All accuracy for unlimited scale
Storage space for network bandwidth
Nothing — it's always exact
questions.explanation: ANN indexes intentionally skip parts of the search space, so they may miss some true nearest neighbors in exchange for much faster queries.

questions.prompt: Why does HNSW typically use more memory than IVF?
options: It stores a full graph of neighbor links across multiple layers
It duplicates the entire dataset three times
It requires a separate database for each layer
It stores raw text instead of embeddings
questions.explanation: HNSW's layered graph structure keeps explicit neighbor links per node, which costs more memory than IVF's compact cluster assignments.

questions.prompt: You're building a cost-sensitive index over 2 billion embeddings with relaxed latency needs. Which is the more natural starting point?
options: IVF (often combined with product quantization)
HNSW with maximum ef_construction
Brute-force exact search
A single flat in-memory array
questions.explanation: IVF's compact clustered structure, often paired with quantization, scales more cheaply to billions of vectors than HNSW's memory-heavy graph.

posts.title: The CAP Theorem, Intuitively
posts.subtitle: Why distributed systems can't have it all
posts.summary: Under a network partition, a distributed system must choose between consistency and availability — you can't guarantee both.

slides.title: CAP Theorem
slides.body: Pick two: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.

slides.title: The three letters
bullets: Consistency: every read sees the latest write
Availability: every request gets a response
Partition tolerance: survives dropped messages

slides.title: Partitions aren't optional
slides.body: Networks fail, so P is mandatory. The real choice is C vs A during a partition.

slides.title: Real systems
bullets: CP: HBase, etcd, ZooKeeper
AP: Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak
Tunable: Cosmos DB

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: CAP is about behavior during partitions
CP refuses to serve stale data
AP stays up and reconciles later

questions.prompt: During a partition, CAP forces a choice between…
options: Speed and cost
Consistency and availability
Latency and throughput
SQL and NoSQL
questions.explanation: P is a given on real networks, so you trade C against A.

posts.title: Database Indexes Without the Mystery
posts.subtitle: Why a B-tree turns a scan into a lookup
posts.summary: An index is a sorted structure that lets the database jump straight to matching rows instead of scanning the whole table.

slides.title: Database Indexes
slides.body: The difference between O(n) and O(log n) at the table level.

slides.title: Full table scans
slides.body: Without an index, WHERE checks every row on disk.
bullets: Slow on big tables
I/O heavy
Gets worse as data grows

slides.title: The B-tree
slides.body: A balanced, sorted tree lets lookups descend in logarithmic steps.
bullets: Sorted keys
Shallow, wide nodes
Range queries stay cheap

slides.title: Trade-offs
bullets: Faster reads
Slower writes (index upkeep)
Extra storage

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Indexes avoid full scans
B-trees give O(log n) lookups
Every index has a write cost

Queries stop working
Data is lost

posts.title: Idempotency for Reliable APIs
posts.subtitle: Make retries safe by design
posts.summary: An idempotent endpoint produces the same result no matter how many times an identical request is retried — essential for safe distributed systems.

slides.title: Idempotency
slides.body: Retry without fear of double charges.

slides.title: Networks lie
slides.body: A client can't tell a lost response from a failed request, so it retries.
bullets: Duplicate orders
Double charges
Corrupted state

slides.title: Idempotency keys
slides.body: The client sends a unique key; the server stores the result and replays it on retry.
bullets: Client generates a key
Server dedupes by key
Same response returned

slides.title: In the wild
bullets: Stripe idempotency keys
PUT is naturally idempotent
POST needs explicit keys

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Assume every request may retry
Dedupe with idempotency keys
Design writes to be replay-safe

questions.prompt: How do you make a POST safe to retry?
